question:
1. Evaluate
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{1}{x} \frac{a^{x}-1}{a-1}\right]^{1 / x}
\]
where \( a>0, a \neq 1 \).
solution:
Solution. Let
\[
f(x)=\left[\frac{1}{x} \frac{a^{x}-1}{a-1}\right]^{1 / x}
\]

Then for \( x>0 \) and \( a>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(x)=-\frac{\log x}{x}-\frac{\log (a-1)}{x}+\frac{\log \left(a^{x}-1\right)}{x}
\]

As \( x \rightarrow+\infty \),
\[
\frac{\log x}{x} \rightarrow 0, \frac{\log (a-1)}{x} \rightarrow 0
\]
and
\[
\frac{\log \left(a^{x}-1\right)}{x}=\frac{\log \left(1-a^{-x}\right)}{x}+\log a \rightarrow \log a .
\]

Hence \( \log f(x) \rightarrow \log a \).
On the other hand, if \( 0<a<1 \) and \( x>0 \), then
\[
\log f(x)=-\frac{\log x}{x}-\frac{\log (1-a)}{x}+\frac{\log \left(1-a^{x}\right)}{x}
\]
and it is clear that all three terms approach zero as \( x \rightarrow+\infty \).
Since exp is a continuous function, we have
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow+\infty} f(x)=\exp \lim _{x \rightarrow+\infty} \log f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\exp \log a=a & \text { if } a>1 \\
\exp 0=1 & \text { if } 0<a<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

More concisely, \( \lim _{x \rightarrow+\infty} f(x)=\max (a, 1) \).
Remark. It is of some interest to see what happens if \( \lim _{x-\infty} \) is replaced by \( \lim _{x-\infty} \). To do this, note that for \( x<0 \) and \( a>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(x)=-\frac{\log |x|}{x}-\frac{\log (a-1)}{x}+\frac{\log \left(1-a^{x}\right)}{x}
\]
and all three terms have limit zero as \( x \rightarrow-\infty \); since in this case \( 1-a^{x}-1 \).

For \( x<0 \) and \( 0<a<1 \), on the other hand,
\[
\log f(x)=-\frac{\log |x|}{x}-\frac{\log (1-a)}{x}+\frac{\log \left(1-a^{-x}\right)}{x}+\log a
\]
so \( \lim _{x--\infty} f(x)=\log a \).
Hence we have
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow-\infty} f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1 & \text { if } a>1 \\
a & \text { if } 0<a<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

In other words, \( \lim _{x--\infty} f(x)=\min (a, 1) \). In particular \( \lim _{x-\infty} f(x) \neq \) \( \lim _{x \rightarrow-\infty} f(x) \), so that \( \lim _{|x|-\infty} f(x) \) does not exist.

descriptive_long.question:
1. Evaluate
\[
\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{1}{inputvar} \frac{baseconst^{inputvar}-1}{baseconst-1}\right]^{1 / inputvar}
\]
where \( baseconst>0, baseconst \neq 1 \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
f(inputvar)=\left[\frac{1}{inputvar} \frac{baseconst^{inputvar}-1}{baseconst-1}\right]^{1 / inputvar}
\]

Then for \( inputvar>0 \) and \( baseconst>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(inputvar)=-\frac{\log inputvar}{inputvar}-\frac{\log (baseconst-1)}{inputvar}+\frac{\log \left(baseconst^{inputvar}-1\right)}{inputvar}
\]

As \( inputvar \rightarrow+\infty \),
\[
\frac{\log inputvar}{inputvar} \rightarrow 0, \frac{\log (baseconst-1)}{inputvar} \rightarrow 0
\]
and
\[
\frac{\log \left(baseconst^{inputvar}-1\right)}{inputvar}=\frac{\log \left(1-baseconst^{-inputvar}\right)}{inputvar}+\log baseconst \rightarrow \log baseconst .
\]

Hence \( \log f(inputvar) \rightarrow \log baseconst \).
On the other hand, if \( 0<baseconst<1 \) and \( inputvar>0 \), then
\[
\log f(inputvar)=-\frac{\log inputvar}{inputvar}-\frac{\log (1-baseconst)}{inputvar}+\frac{\log \left(1-baseconst^{inputvar}\right)}{inputvar}
\]
and it is clear that all three terms approach zero as \( inputvar \rightarrow+\infty \).
Since exp is a continuous function, we have
\[
\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow+\infty} f(inputvar)=\exp \lim _{inputvar \rightarrow+\infty} \log f(inputvar)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\exp \log baseconst=baseconst & \text { if } baseconst>1 \\
\exp 0=1 & \text { if } 0<baseconst<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

More concisely, \( \lim _{inputvar \rightarrow+\infty} f(inputvar)=\max (baseconst, 1) \).
Remark. It is of some interest to see what happens if \( \lim _{inputvar-\infty} \) is replaced by \( \lim _{inputvar-\infty} \). To do this, note that for \( inputvar<0 \) and \( baseconst>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(inputvar)=-\frac{\log |inputvar|}{inputvar}-\frac{\log (baseconst-1)}{inputvar}+\frac{\log \left(1-baseconst^{inputvar}\right)}{inputvar}
\]
and all three terms have limit zero as \( inputvar \rightarrow-\infty \); since in this case \( 1-baseconst^{inputvar}-1 \).

For \( inputvar<0 \) and \( 0<baseconst<1 \), on the other hand,
\[
\log f(inputvar)=-\frac{\log |inputvar|}{inputvar}-\frac{\log (1-baseconst)}{inputvar}+\frac{\log \left(1-baseconst^{-inputvar}\right)}{inputvar}+\log baseconst
\]
so \( \lim _{inputvar--\infty} f(inputvar)=\log baseconst \).
Hence we have
\[
\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow-\infty} f(inputvar)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1 & \text { if } baseconst>1 \\
baseconst & \text { if } 0<baseconst<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

In other words, \( \lim _{inputvar--\infty} f(inputvar)=\min (baseconst, 1) \). In particular \( \lim _{inputvar-\infty} f(inputvar) \neq \) \( \lim _{inputvar \rightarrow-\infty} f(inputvar) \), so that \( \lim _{|inputvar|-\infty} f(inputvar) \) does not exist.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
1. Evaluate
\[
\lim _{blueberry \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{1}{blueberry} \frac{cloudburst^{blueberry}-1}{cloudburst-1}\right]^{1 / blueberry}
\]
where \( cloudburst>0, cloudburst \neq 1 \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
f(blueberry)=\left[\frac{1}{blueberry} \frac{cloudburst^{blueberry}-1}{cloudburst-1}\right]^{1 / blueberry}
\]

Then for \( blueberry>0 \) and \( cloudburst>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(blueberry)=-\frac{\log blueberry}{blueberry}-\frac{\log (cloudburst-1)}{blueberry}+\frac{\log \left(cloudburst^{blueberry}-1\right)}{blueberry}
\]

As \( blueberry \rightarrow+\infty \),
\[
\frac{\log blueberry}{blueberry} \rightarrow 0, \frac{\log (cloudburst-1)}{blueberry} \rightarrow 0
\]
and
\[
\frac{\log \left(cloudburst^{blueberry}-1\right)}{blueberry}=\frac{\log \left(1-cloudburst^{-blueberry}\right)}{blueberry}+\log cloudburst \rightarrow \log cloudburst .
\]

Hence \( \log f(blueberry) \rightarrow \log cloudburst \).
On the other hand, if \( 0<cloudburst<1 \) and \( blueberry>0 \), then
\[
\log f(blueberry)=-\frac{\log blueberry}{blueberry}-\frac{\log (1-cloudburst)}{blueberry}+\frac{\log \left(1-cloudburst^{blueberry}\right)}{blueberry}
\]
and it is clear that all three terms approach zero as \( blueberry \rightarrow+\infty \).
Since exp is a continuous function, we have
\[
\lim _{blueberry \rightarrow+\infty} f(blueberry)=\exp \lim _{blueberry \rightarrow+\infty} \log f(blueberry)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\exp \log cloudburst=cloudburst & \text { if } cloudburst>1 \\
\exp 0=1 & \text { if } 0<cloudburst<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

More concisely, \( \lim _{blueberry \rightarrow+\infty} f(blueberry)=\max (cloudburst, 1) \).
Remark. It is of some interest to see what happens if \( \lim _{blueberry-\infty} \) is replaced by \( \lim _{blueberry-\infty} \). To do this, note that for \( blueberry<0 \) and \( cloudburst>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(blueberry)=-\frac{\log |blueberry|}{blueberry}-\frac{\log (cloudburst-1)}{blueberry}+\frac{\log \left(1-cloudburst^{blueberry}\right)}{blueberry}
\]
and all three terms have limit zero as \( blueberry \rightarrow-\infty \); since in this case \( 1-cloudburst^{blueberry}-1 \).

For \( blueberry<0 \) and \( 0<cloudburst<1 \), on the other hand,
\[
\log f(blueberry)=-\frac{\log |blueberry|}{blueberry}-\frac{\log (1-cloudburst)}{blueberry}+\frac{\log \left(1-cloudburst^{-blueberry}\right)}{blueberry}+\log cloudburst
\]
so \( \lim _{blueberry--\infty} f(blueberry)=\log cloudburst \).
Hence we have
\[
\lim _{blueberry \rightarrow-\infty} f(blueberry)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1 & \text { if } cloudburst>1 \\
cloudburst & \text { if } 0<cloudburst<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

In other words, \( \lim _{blueberry--\infty} f(blueberry)=\min (cloudburst, 1) \). In particular \( \lim _{blueberry-\infty} f(blueberry) \neq \) \( \lim _{blueberry \rightarrow-\infty} f(blueberry) \), so that \( \lim _{|blueberry|-\infty} f(blueberry) \) does not exist.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
1. Evaluate
\[
\lim _{constant \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{1}{constant} \frac{changing^{constant}-1}{changing-1}\right]^{1 / constant}\]
where \( changing>0, changing \neq 1 \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
f(constant)=\left[\frac{1}{constant} \frac{changing^{constant}-1}{changing-1}\right]^{1 / constant}
\]

Then for \( constant>0 \) and \( changing>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(constant)=-\frac{\log constant}{constant}-\frac{\log (changing-1)}{constant}+\frac{\log \left(changing^{constant}-1\right)}{constant}
\]

As \( constant \rightarrow+\infty \),
\[
\frac{\log constant}{constant} \rightarrow 0, \frac{\log (changing-1)}{constant} \rightarrow 0
\]
and
\[
\frac{\log \left(changing^{constant}-1\right)}{constant}=\frac{\log \left(1-changing^{-constant}\right)}{constant}+\log changing \rightarrow \log changing .
\]

Hence \( \log f(constant) \rightarrow \log changing \).
On the other hand, if \( 0<changing<1 \) and \( constant>0 \), then
\[
\log f(constant)=-\frac{\log constant}{constant}-\frac{\log (1-changing)}{constant}+\frac{\log \left(1-changing^{constant}\right)}{constant}
\]
and it is clear that all three terms approach zero as \( constant \rightarrow+\infty \).
Since exp is a continuous function, we have
\[
\lim _{constant \rightarrow+\infty} f(constant)=\exp \lim _{constant \rightarrow+\infty} \log f(constant)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\exp \log changing=changing & \text { if } changing>1 \\
\exp 0=1 & \text { if } 0<changing<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

More concisely, \( \lim _{constant \rightarrow+\infty} f(constant)=\max (changing, 1) \).
Remark. It is of some interest to see what happens if \( \lim _{constant-\infty} \) is replaced by \( \lim _{constant-\infty} \). To do this, note that for \( constant<0 \) and \( changing>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(constant)=-\frac{\log |constant|}{constant}-\frac{\log (changing-1)}{constant}+\frac{\log \left(1-changing^{constant}\right)}{constant}
\]
and all three terms have limit zero as \( constant \rightarrow-\infty \); since in this case \( 1-changing^{constant}-1 \).

For \( constant<0 \) and \( 0<changing<1 \), on the other hand,
\[
\log f(constant)=-\frac{\log |constant|}{constant}-\frac{\log (1-changing)}{constant}+\frac{\log \left(1-changing^{-constant}\right)}{constant}+\log changing
\]
so \( \lim _{constant--\infty} f(constant)=\log changing \).
Hence we have
\[
\lim _{constant \rightarrow-\infty} f(constant)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1 & \text { if } changing>1 \\
changing & \text { if } 0<changing<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

In other words, \( \lim _{constant--\infty} f(constant)=\min (changing, 1) \). In particular \( \lim _{constant-\infty} f(constant) \neq \) \( \lim _{constant \rightarrow-\infty} f(constant) \), so that \( \lim _{|constant|-\infty} f(constant) \) does not exist.

garbled_string.question:
Evaluate
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} \frac{hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp}-1}{hjgrksla-1}\right]^{1 / qzxwvtnp}
\]
where \( hjgrksla>0, hjgrksla \neq 1 \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
f(qzxwvtnp)=\left[\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} \frac{hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp}-1}{hjgrksla-1}\right]^{1 / qzxwvtnp}
\]

Then for \( qzxwvtnp>0 \) and \( hjgrksla>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(qzxwvtnp)=-\frac{\log qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}-\frac{\log (hjgrksla-1)}{qzxwvtnp}+\frac{\log \left(hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp}-1\right)}{qzxwvtnp}
\]

As \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow+\infty \),
\[
\frac{\log qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp} \rightarrow 0, \frac{\log (hjgrksla-1)}{qzxwvtnp} \rightarrow 0
\]
and
\[
\frac{\log \left(hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp}-1\right)}{qzxwvtnp}=\frac{\log \left(1-hjgrksla^{-qzxwvtnp}\right)}{qzxwvtnp}+\log hjgrksla \rightarrow \log hjgrksla .
\]

Hence \( \log f(qzxwvtnp) \rightarrow \log hjgrksla \).
On the other hand, if \( 0<hjgrksla<1 \) and \( qzxwvtnp>0 \), then
\[
\log f(qzxwvtnp)=-\frac{\log qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}-\frac{\log (1-hjgrksla)}{qzxwvtnp}+\frac{\log \left(1-hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp}\right)}{qzxwvtnp}
\]
and it is clear that all three terms approach zero as \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow+\infty \).
Since exp is a continuous function, we have
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow+\infty} f(qzxwvtnp)=\exp \lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow+\infty} \log f(qzxwvtnp)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\exp \log hjgrksla=hjgrksla & \text { if } hjgrksla>1 \\
\exp 0=1 & \text { if } 0<hjgrksla<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

More concisely, \( \lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow+\infty} f(qzxwvtnp)=\max (hjgrksla, 1) \).
Remark. It is of some interest to see what happens if \( \lim _{qzxwvtnp-\infty} \) is replaced by \( \lim _{qzxwvtnp-\infty} \). To do this, note that for \( qzxwvtnp<0 \) and \( hjgrksla>1 \), we have
\[
\log f(qzxwvtnp)=-\frac{\log |qzxwvtnp|}{qzxwvtnp}-\frac{\log (hjgrksla-1)}{qzxwvtnp}+\frac{\log \left(1-hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp}\right)}{qzxwvtnp}
\]
and all three terms have limit zero as \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow-\infty \); since in this case \( 1-hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp}-1 \).

For \( qzxwvtnp<0 \) and \( 0<hjgrksla<1 \), on the other hand,
\[
\log f(qzxwvtnp)=-\frac{\log |qzxwvtnp|}{qzxwvtnp}-\frac{\log (1-hjgrksla)}{qzxwvtnp}+\frac{\log \left(1-hjgrksla^{-qzxwvtnp}\right)}{qzxwvtnp}+\log hjgrksla
\]
so \( \lim _{qzxwvtnp--\infty} f(qzxwvtnp)=\log hjgrksla \).
Hence we have
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow-\infty} f(qzxwvtnp)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1 & \text { if } hjgrksla>1 \\
 hjgrksla & \text { if } 0<hjgrksla<1
\end{array}\right.
\]

In other words, \( \lim _{qzxwvtnp--\infty} f(qzxwvtnp)=\min (hjgrksla, 1) \). In particular \( \lim _{qzxwvtnp-\infty} f(qzxwvtnp) \neq \) \( \lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow-\infty} f(qzxwvtnp) \), so that \( \lim _{|qzxwvtnp|-\infty} f(qzxwvtnp) \) does not exist.

kernel_variant.question:
For a positive parameter a with a\neq 1, set  
  F_a(x)=  2\sqrt{x}            |a^{\sqrt{x}}-ln x| ^{\,10/\sqrt{x}},  x>1.  
        (x^2+1)(a^2+4)  
Evaluate the limit  
  L(a)=lim_{x\to \infty } F_a(x)  
as an explicit function of a.
kernel_variant.solution:
Put  
 f(x)=  2\sqrt{x} \cdot |a^{\sqrt{x}}-ln x| /(x^2+1)(a^2+4), so F_a(x)=f(x)^{10/\sqrt{x}}.  

Step 1 - logarithm.  
 ln F_a(x)=10/\sqrt{x}\cdot [ln 2+\frac{1}{2} ln x-ln(x^2+1)-ln(a^2+4)+ln|a^{\sqrt{x}}-ln x|].

Step 2 - dominant factor inside |\cdot |.  

(i) a>1.  For large x, a^{\sqrt{x}}\gg ln x, hence |a^{\sqrt{x}}-ln x|=a^{\sqrt{x}}-ln x>0 and  

 ln|a^{\sqrt{x}}-ln x|=ln(a^{\sqrt{x}})+ln(1-ln x\cdot a^{-\sqrt{x}})  
           =\sqrt{x} ln a+o(1).  

(ii) 0<a<1.  Now a^{\sqrt{x}}\to 0 while ln x\to \infty , so |a^{\sqrt{x}}-ln x|=ln x-a^{\sqrt{x}}. Consequently  

 ln|a^{\sqrt{x}}-ln x|=ln ln x+ln(1-a^{\sqrt{x}}/ln x)=ln ln x+o(1).  

Step 3 - the small baggage.  
Because ln x/\sqrt{x}\to 0 and every constant divided by \sqrt{x} vanishes, we have  

 10/\sqrt{x}\cdot [ln 2+\frac{1}{2} ln x-ln(x^2+1)-ln(a^2+4)]=O(ln x/\sqrt{x})\to 0.  

Step 4 - assemble the leading term.  

(i) a>1: ln F_a(x)=10/\sqrt{x}\cdot (\sqrt{x} ln a+o(1))+o(1)=10 ln a.  

(ii) 0<a<1: ln F_a(x)=10/\sqrt{x}\cdot (ln ln x+o(1))+o(1)\to 0.  

Step 5 - back to the original scale.  
 L(a)=exp lim_{x\to \infty } ln F_a(x)=  
  { a^{10}, a>1;  
   1,   0<a<1. }  

Equivalently, L(a)=max{1,a^{10}}.  

Remark.  The borderline case a=1 was excluded because the expression |a^{\sqrt{x}}-ln x| would then simplify to |1-ln x|, changing the leading behaviour; one checks separately that F_1(x)\to 1.

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
